Learn more: [PMC Disclaimer](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/disclaimer/) | [PMC Copyright Notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/) ![Image 14: Stem Cell Research logo](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/banners/logo-pheelsevier.png) Stem Cell Res . 2020 Dec 9;50:102115. doi: [10.1016/j.scr.2020.102115](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scr.2020.102115) * [Search in PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/search/?term=%22Stem%20Cell%20Res%22[jour]) * [Search in PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Stem%20Cell%20Res%22[jour]) * [View in NLM Catalog](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/nlmcatalog?term=%22Stem%20Cell%20Res%22[Title%20Abbreviation]) * [Add to search](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551?term=%22Stem%20Cell%20Res%22[jour]) # Furin cleavage sites naturally occur in coronaviruses [Yiran Wu](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Wu%20Y%22[Author]) ### Yiran Wu a iHuman Institute, ShanghaiTech University, Shanghai 201210, China Find articles by [Yiran Wu](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Wu%20Y%22[Author]) a, [Suwen Zhao](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Zhao%20S%22[Author]) ### Suwen Zhao a iHuman Institute, ShanghaiTech University, Shanghai 201210, China b School of Life Science and Technology, ShanghaiTech University, Shanghai 201210, China Find articles by [Suwen Zhao](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Zhao%20S%22[Author]) a b * Author information * Article notes * Copyright and License information a iHuman Institute, ShanghaiTech University, Shanghai 201210, China b School of Life Science and Technology, ShanghaiTech University, Shanghai 201210, China Received 2020 Sep 30; Revised 2020 Dec 2; Accepted 2020 Dec 4 © 2020 The Author(s) Since January 2020 Elsevier has created a COVID-19 resource centre with free information in English and Mandarin on the novel coronavirus COVID-19. The COVID-19 resource centre is hosted on Elsevier Connect, the company's public news and information website. Elsevier hereby grants permission to make all its COVID-19-related research that is available on the COVID-19 resource centre - including this research content - immediately available in PubMed Central and other publicly funded repositories, such as the WHO COVID database with rights for unrestricted research re-use and analyses in any form or by any means with acknowledgement of the original source. These permissions are granted for free by Elsevier for as long as the COVID-19 resource centre remains active. [PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/) PMCID: PMC7836551 PMID: [33340798](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33340798/) ## [](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551)Abstract ## [](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551)Abstract The spike protein is a focused target of COVID-19, a pandemic caused by SARS-CoV-2. A 12-nt insertion at S1/S2 in the spike coding sequence yields a furin cleavage site, which raised controversy views on origin of the virus. Here we analyzed the phylogenetic relationships of coronavirus spike proteins and mapped furin recognition motif on the tree. Furin cleavage sites occurred independently for multiple times in the evolution of the coronavirus family, supporting the natural occurring hypothesis of SARS-CoV-2. ## [](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551)Introduction 1 The coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) pandemic, caused by coronavirus SARS-CoV-2, is still rapidly spreading. Scientists all around the world are studying the infection process of the virus and looking for therapeutic solutions to prevent and cure the disease. Spike protein is one of most important targets in COVID-19 research, not only in mechanism study but also in vaccine development and therapeutic antibody design ([Premkumar et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0005), [Abraham, 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0010)). The spike protein forms a homotrimer ([Fig. 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0005) A) and protrudes outside the membrane of the virion ([Li, 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0015), [Wrapp et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0020), [Walls et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0025)). It binds to angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) on human cell surface, undergoes conformational changes, and mediates the fusion of the virion to human cells ([Li et al., 2003](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0030), [Yan et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0035), [Lan et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0040), [Wang et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0045), [Benton et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0050)).Fig. 1Structure of SARS-CoV-2 spike protein (PDB ID: 6VYB ([Walls et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0025)). A) Spike homotrimer in open conformation. B) Cleavage sites on spike protein (marked by arrows). Red cartoon, S1; light/dark blue cartoon, S2; dark blue cartoon, S2′). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.) The spike protein consists of two subunits: the N-terminal S1 subunit, containing the receptor binding domain that interacts with ACE2 and induces conformational change; while the C-terminal S2 subunit, containing the fusion peptide that is responsible for fusing the membranes of virion and human cells ([Li, 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0015)). To function properly, the spike protein needs to be cleaved by host proteases to separate the two subunits ([Simmons et al., 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0055)). Study of SARS-CoV (caused the severe acute respiratory syndrome in 2002–2003 and belonged to the same species as SARS-CoV-2) showed that cleavage at S1/S2 enhances fusogenicity of spike protein ([Bosch et al., 2003](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0060)). Another cleavage site producing S2′ (fusion peptide and the C-terminal region of S2) was also identified ([Fig. 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0005)B) ([Belouzard et al., 2009](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0065)). The S2′ site is not far from the S1/S2 site, and cleavage either or both them can yield the separation of two subunits of spike. For coronaviruses, multiple proteases were reported responsible for the cleavage, including TMPRSS2 ([Hoffmann et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0070), [Glowacka et al., 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0075)); cathepsin CTSL ([Bosch et al., 2008](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0080), [Ou et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0085)), and trypsin ([Belouzard et al., 2009](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0065), [Bertram et al., 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0090)). The spike protein of the Middle East respiratory syndrome coronavirus (MERS-CoV) was discovered to be more effectively cleaved by another protease, furin ([Millet and Whittaker, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0095)). In MERS-CoV, both S1/S2 and S2′ sites have sequence RXXR, the furin recognition motif. In human body, furin is ubiquitously expressed ([Braun and Sauter, 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0100)). This may explain the highly lethal nature and high rate of multiple system failure caused by MERS ([Millet and Whittaker, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0095)). Surprisingly, SARS-CoV-2 has the furin recognition motif at S1/S2, causing by a 12-nucleotide insertion not presented even in its closest relatives ([Walls et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0025), [Coutard et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0105)). This stimulates a conspiracy that this furin site can only be manual work, thus SARS-CoV-2 must be created in a laboratory. Here, we analyzed the sequences of coronaviruses and found furin sites occurred independently for multiple times during evolution. This exhibits natural occurrence of furin cleavage site in SARS-CoV-2 spike protein is highly possible. Thus, the insertion of furin cleavage site into SARS-CoV-2 spike protein is not necessarily a result of manual work. ## [](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551)Results 2 ### Phylogenetic tree of spike protein sequences reveals major groups of coronaviruses 2.1 Coronaviruses are members of the subfamily _Orthocoronavinae_ (also named _Coronavinae_) in the family _Coronavidae_. There are four genera: _Alphacoronavirus_, _Betacoronavirus_, _Gammacoronavirus_, and _Deltacoronavirus_. The first two genera only infect mammals, while the last two primarily infect birds but also mammals ([Cui et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0110)). We collected sequences of coronavirus spike proteins from the InterPro database ([Mitchell et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0115)) and performed phylogenetic analysis. The phylogenetic tree ([Fig. 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0010) A) generally matches reported relationship of coronaviruses ([Cui et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0110)): three genera (_Betacoronavirus_; _Gammacoronavirus_, and _Deltacoronavirus_) each forms a single clade, while only _Alphacoronavirus_ has a small group left outside (see [Fig. 5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0025) for representative sequences). The left-out _Alphacoronavirus_ group contains _Rhinolophus_ bat coronavirus HKU2 that was reported to be closely related to two typical _Alphacoronavirus_, human coronavirus NL63 and human coronavirus 229E, in a phylogenetic analysis based on the RNA-dependent RNA polymerase coding region ([Cui et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0110)). We aligned the whole genomes of the three viruses and found _Rhinolophus_ bat coronavirus HKU2 is quite unique in its spike protein-coding region ([Fig. S1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#s0095)). Such result explains why this clade was separated from other _Alphacoronavirus_ in our phylogenetic analysis.Fig. 2Phylogenetic tree of coronavirus spike protein sequences. A) Noting genera of coronavirus. B) Subtree of _Betacoronavirus_, noting subgenera. Furthermore, the five subgenera of _Betacoronavirus_ were nicely displayed as monophyletic ([Fig. 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0010)B): _Sarbecovirus_ (e.g. SARS-CoV-2 and SARS-CoV), _Merbecovirus_ (e.g. MERS-CoV), _Embecovirus_ (e.g. human coronavirus OC43 and human coronavirus HKU1, both causing common cold), and two small subgenera _Hibecovirus_ and _Nobecovirus_. _Hibecovirus_ and _Nobecovirus_ are only discovered in bats so far, and our phylogenetic analysis shows they are related to _Sarbecovirus_. ### Furin cleavage site at spike S1/S2 also occurs in a virus close to Sarbecovirus 2.2 We mapped the furin recognition motif RXXR at both S1/S2 and S2′ positions in phylogenetic trees of spike protein sequences ([Fig. 3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0015), [Fig. 4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0020), [Fig. 5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0025) and [S2,](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#s0095) S3). In the _Sarbecovirus_+_Hibecovirus_+_Nobecovirus_ clade ([Fig. 3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0015)), furin cleavage sites at either position occur only in limited ranges. Strains of SARS-CoV-2 (we also added sequences from the GISAID database) have furin cleavage sites at spike S1/S2. Moreover, SARS-CoV-2 is the only virus in subgenus _Sarbecovirus_ having this feature, while even its closest relatives, bat coronavirus RaTG13 (sequence identity 97.7%) and pangolin coronaviruses (92.9%–90.7%), do not have furin site. However, in _Hibecovirus_, the sister clade of _Sarbecovirus_, a _Hipposideros_ bat coronavirus collected in 2013 at Zhejiang Province in China has furin site at S1/S2. Interestingly, the other member in _Hibecovirus_ lacks such site, similar to the situation of SARS-CoV-2 and its close relatives. The SARS-CoV-2 strains and _Hipposideros_ bat coronavirus (Zhejiang 2013) are not sister groups, in agreement with the distinct sequence patterns of their furin cleavage sites at spike S1/S2 ([Fig. 6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0030) A). Besides, two strains of bat coronavirus HKU9, belonging to _Nobecovirus_, are the only members in the _Sarbecovirus_+_Hibecovirus_+_Nobecovirus_ clade having furin cleavage site at spike S2′.Fig. 3Mapping of furin recognition motif on phylogenetic tree of spike protein sequences, the _Sarbecovirus+Hibecovirus+Nobecovirus_ clade. Sequences in the SARS-CoV-2 clade were clustered with 95% identity threshold; in the SARS-CoV-2 clade were clustered with 99% identity threshold.Fig. 4Mapping of furin recognition motif on phylogenetic tree of spike protein sequences, A) _Merbecovirus_ (for uncompressed tree see [Fig. S2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#s0095)) and B) _Embecovirus_ (for uncompressed tree see [Fig. S3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#s0095)).Fig. 5Mapping of furin recognition motif on phylogenetic tree of spike protein sequences, the _Alphacoronavirus_+_Gammacoronavirus_+_Deltacoronavirus_ clade. Sequences clustered with 95% identity threshold.Fig. 6Positions of furin cleavage sites at the linking region of S1 and S2. A) Multiple sequence alignment of representative _Betacoronavirus_ spike protein S1/S2 region, with furin recognition motifs highlighted (red colorboxes in sequence alignment). Phylogenetic tree of spike protein sequences is colored to indicate subgenera (coloring scheme the same as in [Fig. 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0010)B). B) Positions of furin cleavage sites in different coronavirus genera (red cartoon, furin recognition motif; red arrow, cleavage site); structures: SARS-CoV-2, PDB ID 6VYB ([Walls et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0025)), with missing loop added; feline coronavirus (FCoV) UU16, homology model based on PDB ID 5SZS ([Walls et al., 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0195)); infectious bronchitis coronavirus (IBV), PDB ID 6CV0 ([Shang et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0205)). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.) ### Furin cleavage sites are common in Betacoronavirus 2.3 Our mapping results showed that the furin recognition motif is more common in _Merbecovirus_ and _Embecovirus_ ([Figs. 4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0020) and [S2,](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#s0095) S3). In _Merbecovirus_, furin sites at spike S1/S2 occur in three clades: MERS-CoV strains, the bat coronavirus HKU5 strains, and coronavirus Neoromicia/PML-PHE1/RSA/2011 with its relatives ([Figs. 4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0020)A and [S2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#s0095)). Besides, MERS-CoV and bat coronavirus HKU5 are the only clades in _Merbecovirus_ having furin cleavage site at S2′. In _Embecovirus_, furin recognition motif at spike S1/S2 is universal: All strains but a few exceptions have furin cleavage sites at spike S1/S2 ([Figs. 4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0020)B and [S3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#s0095)). Interestingly, the Longquan Aa mouse coronavirus ([Wang et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0120)) loses this furin site, while its close relatives (e.g. China Rattus coronavirus HKU24, sequence identity 96.0%) maintains the furin cleavage site. This provides an example of naturally occurred sequence variation at spike S1/S2 among closely related coronaviruses. Besides, for spike S2′, only several single strains have furin recognition motif ([Fig. S3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#s0095)). ### Furin cleavage sites also occur in other genera of coronavirus 2.4 Our mapping results showed furin cleavage sites are widely present in the whole coronavirus family ([Fig. 5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0025)). For spike S1/S2, furin recognition motif is universal in _Gammacoronavirus_, and also occurs in two clades of _Alphacorovanvirus_: feline coronavirus and relatives, and Chevrier's field mouse coronavirus. For spike S2′, furin recognition motif occurs in several independent clades, covering all the three genera. Notably, in the two human coronaviruses in _Alphacoronavirus_ causing common cold, HCoV NL63 has furin cleavage site at spike S2′, while the HCoV 229E (protein sequence identity 63.8%) lacks such feature. ### Furin cleavages sites occurred independently for six times in Betacoronavirus 2.5 The alignment of linking regions of spike S1 and S2 domains in representative _Betacoronavirus_ ([Fig. 6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0030)A) shows this region is less conserved than the neighboring folded S1 and S2 segments. Within a subgenus the sequences are well aligned, but among subgenera the similarity is low. The furin cleavage site of SARS-CoV-2 spike S1/S2 is formed by a insertion of PRRA in comparison to other _Sarbecovirus_ including close relative RaTG13, showing it occurred very recently and independently. Similarly, _Hipposideros_ bat coronavirus (Zhejiang 2013) in _Hibecovirus_ has furin site of independent origin, though the occurring time is hard to decide for in this subgenera only two sequences were published. _Merbecovirus_ and _Embecovirus_ both have multiple coronavirus species with furin cleavage sites at spike S1/S2, but their situations are different: In _Merbecovirus_, furin cleavage sites prevail in three non-sister clades ([Figs. 4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0020)A and [6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0030)A). Moreover, the positions of furin recognition motifs in the linking regions are unique to each clade, as exhibited in alignments of both protein sequences ([Fig. 6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0030)A) and nucleotide sequences ([Fig. S4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#s0095)A). These indicated for of the three clades in _Merbecovirus_, furin cleavage sites have an independent origin. In _Embecovirus_, to the contrast, all the furin cleavage sites are variations based on a 5-residue region with consensus sequence RRXRR. The region is well aligned in both protein and nucleotide sequences ([Figs. 6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0030)A and [S4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#s0095)B). This suggested the furin cleavage sites of _Embecovirus_ share a common ancestor. In addition, in _Alphacoronavirus_ and _Gammacoronavirus_, S1/S2 cleavage sites reside at a different loop comparing to the site in _Betacoronavirus_ ([Fig. 6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#f0030)B), therefore furin cleavage sites at spike S1/S2 in these two genera occurred independently from those in _Betacoronavirus_ in evolution. ## [](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551)Discussion 3 Furin cleavage is critical to many viral diseases, including HIV, Ebola, and influenza H5 and H7 ([Becker et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0125)). Furin is a ubiquitously expressed protease. In human body, it has a wider distribution range than the major protease responsible for cleaving spike, TMPRSS2 ([Fig. S5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#s0095)). Therefore, coronaviruses with spike containing furin cleavage site may have advantage in spreading. Deletions of furin cleavage site in SARS-CoV-2 attenuates replication on respiratory cells ([Johnson et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0130)) and pathogenesis in hamster ([Johnson et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0130), [Lau et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0135)). Furin inhibitors suppress virus production and cytopathic effects in kidney cells ([Cheng et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0140)). Natural polymorphisms losing furin recognition motif in SARS-CoV-2 spike S1/S2 are observed, but very rare ([Xing et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0145)). Variations in this region are more common in viruses cultured _in vitro_ than viruses isolated from clinical samples, suggesting this cleavage site is under selection pressure in human body ([Lau et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0135), [Liu et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0150)). Our analysis exhibits furin cleavage sites at spike S1/S2 occurred independently for several times in coronavirus. Consequently, natural occurring of the site in SARS-CoV-2 is highly possible. This is further supported by other observed natural variations at the linking region of S1 and S2: A natural insertion in SARS-CoV spike though not related to furin recognition motif was reported ([Zhou et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0155)). In _Embecovirus_; Longquan Aa mouse coronavirus ([Wang et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0120)) has a frameshift mutation led to the loss of furin recognition motif ([Fig. S4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#s0095)B); Some strains of murine coronavirus lose furin recognition motif through substitution mutations ([Fig. S3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#s0095)), e.g. in MHV-2 ([Yamada et al., 1997](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0160)). Further study of losing the furin cleavage site in _Embecovirus_ would help to interpret the S1/S2 cleavage of _Betacoronaviruses_. Besides, independent occurrences of furin cleavage sites in surface glycoproteins are not unique to coronavirus: for the hemagglutinin of influenza, only H5 and H7 have furin cleavage sites ([Bottcher-Friebertshauser et al., 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0165)); and these subtypes are distant in phylogenetic tree ([Fig. S6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#s0095)). ## [](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551)Conclusion 4 Furin cleavage sites in spike proteins naturally occurred independently for multiple times in coronaviruses. Such feature of SARS-CoV-2 spike protein is not necessarily a product of manual intervention, though our observation does not rule out the lab-engineered scenario. ## [](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551)Methods 5 ### Sequences of spike proteins in coronaviruses 5.1 Most sequences were obtained from the InterPro database ([Mitchell et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0115)). All the sequences sharing the same domain annotation SAR2-CoV-2 or SAR2-CoV (entries: IPR002552, IPR018548, IPR036326, and IPR042578) were selected and then filtered in three steps: 1) Sequences shorter than 1000 amino acids or not starting with methionine were dropped; 2) The rest sequences were put into multiple sequence alignment and phylogenetic analysis, and a few sequences were found to be out of the coronavirus family thus were dropped; 3) Duplicate sequences were removed using the software CD-HIT version 4.8.1 ([Fu et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0170), [Li and Godzik, 2006](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0175)). Finally spike protein sequences from pangolin coronaviruses, closely related to SARS-CoV-2 but not included in InterPro database, were added in to the sequence set. Genomes of bat coronavirus RmYN02 and pangolin covonaviruses were obtained from GISAID database ([https://www.gisaid.org/](https://www.gisaid.org/)), and spike protein sequences were generated using NCBI ORFfinder server ([https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/orffinder/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/orffinder/)). Additional sequences of SARS-CoV-2 spike proteins were also obtained from GISAID database (at Sep. 29, 2020); Sequences with 25 or more continuous unreadable amino acids were dropped, and 93,420 spike protein sequences were used for analysis. Nucleotide sequences of representative spike sequences were obtained from NCBI nucleotide database ([https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/nuccore/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/nuccore/)). ### Sequence clustering 5.2 Sequence set were clustered using the software CD-HIT version 4.8.1 ([Fu et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0170), [Li and Godzik, 2006](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0175)). ### Multiple sequence alignment 5.3 Multiple sequence alignment jobs were performed using the MAFFT software version 7.299b ([Katoh and Standley, 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0180)). The strategy FFT-NS-2 was selected. For other parameters, the default values were used. The alignment figures in [Fig. S4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#s0095) were generated using the ESPript 3.0 website ([http://espript.ibcp.fr/ESPript/ESPript/](http://espript.ibcp.fr/ESPript/ESPript/)) ([Robert and Gouet, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0185)). ### Phylogenetic analysis 5.4 The phylogenetic trees were generated from multiple sequence alignment results of spike proteins using the MegaX software version 10.1.7 ([Kumar et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0190)). The algorithm Neighbor Joining was selected, and for all parameters the default values were used. ### Molecular modeling 5.5 Homology model of feline coronavirus UU16 was build based on electron-microscopy structure of human coronavirus NL63 spike protein (PDB ID: 5SZS ([Walls et al., 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0195)) using the Prime program version 4.2 ([Schrödinger, 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0200)) in Schrodinger software platform. Missing loops of SARS-CoV-2 spike protein was modeled also using the Prime program based on structure with PDB ID: 6VYB ([Walls et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551#b0025)). ## [](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551)CRediT authorship contribution statement **Yiran Wu:** Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. **Suwen Zhao:** Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. ## [](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551)Declaration of Competing Interest The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper. ## [](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551)Acknowledgement We thank 10.13039/501100012600ShanghaiTech University for supporting this work. ## [](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7836551)Supplementary data Appendix A The following are the Supplementary data to this article:Supplementary Fig. S1Slide-window Simplot graph of _Rhinolophus_ bat coronavirus HKU2, human coronavirus NL63, and human coronavirus 229E. sources.text: ![Virology Blog - About Viruses and Viral Disease](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![Virology Blog - About Viruses and Viral Disease](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![Virology Blog - About Viruses and Viral Disease](https://virology.ws/wp-content/uploads/2021/01/cropped-cropped-blog-new-2021-no-names-3-350x93.jpg) ![Virology Blog - About Viruses and Viral Disease](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![Virology Blog - About Viruses and Viral Disease](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![Virology Blog - About Viruses and Viral Disease](https://virology.ws/wp-content/uploads/2021/01/cropped-cropped-blog-new-2021-no-names-3-350x93.jpg) # SARS-CoV-2 furin cleavage site revisited The spike glycoprotein of SARS-CoV-2 contains a cleavage site for host cell proteases called furins. Deciphering the role of this cleavage site during infection is important for understanding the origin of the pandemic virus and its disease pattern in humans. Back in February it was not known if the furin site in the SARS-CoV-2 was cleaved by cell proteases, and whether its presence is required for infectivity. Both questions have [now been answered](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/32362314). ![Spike cleavage sites](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)![Spike cleavage sites](https://www.virology.ws/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/Screenshot-2020-05-14-20.28.15-1024x315.png) ![Spike cleavage sites](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![Spike cleavage sites](https://www.virology.ws/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/Screenshot-2020-05-14-20.28.15-1024x315.png) The figure shows amino acids at cleavage sites in the spike glycoproteins of various CoVs. A furin site is present in the spike glycoprotein of HCoV-OC43, HCoV-HKU1, MERS-CoV and SARS-CoV 2. It is called a multibasic site because it contains multiple basic (arginine) amino acids. The spike glycoproteins of HCoV-NL63, HCoV-229E, and SARS-CoV do not contain this multibasic cleavage site. Neither do SARS-related CoVs found in bats, including RaTG13, the virus with the closest overall genome sequence identity with SARS-CoV-2. To study cleavage and function of the furin site, various CoV spike glycoproteins were engineered into vesicular stomatitis virus particles. This manipulation allowed the study of infected cells without the need for a BSL3 facility. The spike glycoprotein of SARS-CoV-2 was efficiently cleaved, while that of SARS-CoV or RaTG13 was not. Furthermore, when the SARS-CoV-2 furin site was exchanged with the corresponding sequence from SARS-CoV or RaTG13, no cleavage was observed. That cleavage was mediated by furins was verified by using specific protease inhibitors. Cleavage of CoV spike glycoproteins is required for fusion of the viral and cell membranes upon entry. VSV harboring the spike of SARS-CoV-2 caused fusion of a human lung cell line; substitution of the furin cleavage site with the corresponding sequence from SARS-CoV or RaTG13 prevented cell fusion. However, VSV harboring the spike of SARS-CoV did cause fusion of these lung cells, due to cleavage by a different protease. These observations demonstrate that the furin cleavage site in the spike glycoprotein is essential for entry of SARS-CoV-2 into lung cells. In contrast, a monobasic cleavage site is sufficient for entry of SARS-CoV. The activation of the spike glycoproteins of SARS-CoV-2 and MERS-CoV are therefore similar. They both must first be cleaved by furins followed by cleavage by a different cell protease, TMPRSS2. An interesting question is the origin of the furin cleavage site it SARS-CoV-2. Its closest relative, the bat isolate RaTG13, does not have this site. Nor do any of the other bat SARS-like CoVs or the pangolin CoVs that have been isolated. However recently a newly isolated bat SARS-like CoV, RmYN02, was shown to [contain a poly basic amino acid insertion](http://www.cell.com/current-biology/retrieve/pii/S096098222030662X?_returnURL=https%3A%2F%2Flinkinghub.elsevier.com%2Fretrieve%2Fpii%2FS096098222030662X%3Fshowall%3Dtrue) in the spike glycoprotein. This observation supports the hypothesis that the furin cleavage site in SARS-CoV-2 arose by recombination among bat viruses in nature. ### 18 thoughts on “SARS-CoV-2 furin cleavage site revisited” Pingback: [SARS-CoV-2 furin cleavage site revisited – Virology Hub](http://achengula.com/2020/05/15/sars-cov-2-furin-cleavage-site-revisited/) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/733e76b06db1b99819e6d0c05f784e02?s=50&d=mm&r=g) Vincent Racaniello said: > This observation supports the hypothesis that the furin cleavage site in SARS-CoV-2 arose by recombination among bat viruses in nature. A recombination event is key to this hypothesis. In nature, it requires that a single animal was simultaneously infected by two viruses; a low probability but not implausible event. Recombination in the lab is also plausible but it requires Gain-of-Function type research on the RaTG13 virus. I have no idea how probable this lab scenario is but I’d prefer to see the lab hypothesis falsified rather than dismissed outright. Bret Weinstein on his Darkhorse podcast thinks the lab hypothesis is much more likely but I think he is overly focused the respiratory infection of cave roosting bats rather than the gastrointestinal infection of bats that spread the disease to other animals through their guano outside of the cave environment. Horseshoe bats practice “perch feeding” which means that nocturnal animals in the bat’s feeding ground are most likely to encounter infected guano; pangolins, civets, and racoon dogs fit the bill and are in high demand in the black market for exotic animals. The Mekong River basin is ground zero for this kind of doubly-infected recombination event. ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/761cfb7cc3d1b538b9c115a4c1f1fcbc?s=50&d=mm&r=g) Vincent – I don’t understand your logic here. You’re basically saying a virus that shares 61% similarity (RmYN02) is a smoking gun to a natural event simply because it also shares the insertion of multiple amino acids at the junction site of the S1 and S2? You realize this virus is something on the order of 11500 bp different from SARS-CoV-2…hardly the precursor virus. ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/0c860e731ea0ddccdce3b804649d0072?s=50&d=mm&r=g) Flyby News has posted a link to this page and to this presentation by Chris Martenson which supports the premise above. Martenson condenses his critique to a single question: â€œHow did that polybasic furin cleavage site PRRA get into Covid 19?â€ ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/7c5555a68841b01f5e46b545ea4a6470?s=50&d=mm&r=g) Took a look at Flyby News. Appears to be a large scale compendium of conspiracy theories: JFK, 9-11, NASA, you name it. ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/7c5555a68841b01f5e46b545ea4a6470?s=50&d=mm&r=g) E. Braun, D. Sauter. Furin-mediated protein processing in infectious diseases and cancer, August, 2019. <https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/pdf/10.1002/cti2.1073> Current review of (suddenly more important) therapeutic inhibition of furin. The GÃ¶ttingen paper confirms that without the furin clip, SARS-CoV-2 cannot infect human lung cells. ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/7c5555a68841b01f5e46b545ea4a6470?s=50&d=mm&r=g) <https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5086990/> V. helpful PNAS paper from 2016 on the timing, sites and mechanisms of MERS infection of lung tissue. Note that for MERS, the furin protease not only enables lung cell infection — it puts it on a fast track. See Fig. 7, for example. ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/7c5555a68841b01f5e46b545ea4a6470?s=50&d=mm&r=g) Hypoxia-enhanced Expression of the Proprotein Convertase Furin Is Mediated by Hypoxia-inducible Factor-1 <https://www.jbc.org/content/280/8/6561.full.pdf+html> There is a reluctance to use furin inhibitors as therapeutic agents because furin is a vital enzyme with hundreds of known substrates. However, when furin levels are much elevated, as they are in hypoxia, there might be some room to work. This paper reports experiments in which the investigators determined that hypoxia, on average, tripled the expression of furin. Hypoxia-inducible factor-1 (HIF-1), a transcription complex, apparently produces the effect. The advantage to SARS-CoV-2 seems clear. The furin clip is the rate-limiting step in the infection of human lung cells. As the infection progresses in the lungs, access to oxygen diminishes and furin becomes more and more abundantly available. A self-reinforcing spiral. The authors also add some remarks about cytokines: â€œâ€¦furin regulation by HIF-1 may be a more generalized phenomenon that could apply to other cellular contexts, including inflammatory condition, where furin activity was shown to be up-regulated (42).â€ Could a therapeutic target be HIF-1? One of furinâ€™s three promoters, P1, is known to be triggered by various cytokines, including interferon gamma, interleukins, and others. Net, there might be another self-reinforcing spiral involving cytokines. ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/7c5555a68841b01f5e46b545ea4a6470?s=50&d=mm&r=g) Why hydroxyhloroquine didnâ€™t work. Chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine are thought to operate in the endolysosomal entry pathway into cells. Glance at the MERS infection entry pathways â€“ early and late. The endosome pathway is a late entry pathway. SARS-CoV-2 probably doesnâ€™t use it. The furin clip enables the early action of a second protease, TMPRSS2, which in turn enables the virus to fuse directly with the target cell — and dump its RNA into the cytoplasm. This is a shortcut past the late entry pathway, i.e., the endosome/lysosome pathway. Net, chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine work in a pathway that is simply bypassed — a direct consequence of the furin clip. The TMPRSS2 inhibitor camostat is approved in Japan. Both camostat and the related agent nafamostat are in clinical trials as treatments for COVID-19 in the Netherlands and Germany. There are no approved furin inhibitors I am aware of, but one might ultimately think about a combination treatment including both furin and TMPRSS2 protease inhibitors. Pingback: [Computing Forever interview with Dolores Cahill contains numerous inaccuracies about COVID-19 and vaccines - Health Feedback](https://healthfeedback.org/computing-forever-interview-with-dolores-cahill-contains-numerous-inaccuracies-about-covid-19-and-vaccines/) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/69bb38308f32ea60b9b9e7d00839a6f6?s=50&d=mm&r=g) @John, potential explanations of the following observation? Pseudovirus Entry of SARS-CoV-2 S-fur/mut (furin site removed) is 1.5-fold higher than SARS-CoV-2 S. See Figure 1A. ACE2 is a functional receptro for SARS-CoV-2 S protein. Entry of MLV pseudotyped with SARS-CoV-2 S, SARS-CoV S and SARS-CoV-2 S-fur/mut in VeroE6 cells. Walls et al., 2020, Cell 180, 281â€“292 Elsevier Inc. <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cell.2020.02.058>. ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/7c5555a68841b01f5e46b545ea4a6470?s=50&d=mm&r=g) The furin clip enables SARS-CoV-2 to infect human lung cells. It may infect other types of cells, possibly using other pathways and other clips at different rates. The experiments described in Fig 1a of the Seattle paper targeted VeroE6 and BHK kidney cell lines from monkeys and hamsters. The â€œâ€¦ results suggest that S1/S2 cleavage during S biosynthesis was not necessary for S-mediated entry in the conditions of our experiments.â€ I donâ€™t see a way to overlay these kidney cell studies on the processes of human lung cell infection by SARS-CoV-2, in which S1/S2 cleavage by furin is (we learned on April 28) essential. The authors speculated that the value of the polybasic furin cleavage site in SARS-CoV-2 could be â€œto expand its tropism and/or enhance its transmissibilityâ€ over that of SARS. This turned out to be correct. ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/dfa52e56174bbb6a6600129fa3435ec8?s=50&d=mm&r=g) Can the furin cleavage insertion been accomplished using Crispr/Cas? ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/2dbd6121bc0160a1ecbc7185470a4fc7?s=50&d=mm&r=g) The following statement is false: However recently a newly isolated bat SARS-like CoV, RmYN02, was shown to contain a poly basic amino acid insertion in the spike glycoprotein. This observation supports the hypothesis that the furin cleavage site in SARS-CoV-2 arose by recombination among bat viruses in nature. SARS-Cov-2 has a furin sequence P-R-R-A. The RmYN02 sequence has NO BASIN AMINO ACIDS!!! ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/2dbd6121bc0160a1ecbc7185470a4fc7?s=50&d=mm&r=g) The following statement is false: However recently a newly isolated bat SARS-like CoV, RmYN02, was shown to contain a poly basic amino acid insertion in the spike glycoprotein. This observation supports the hypothesis that the furin cleavage site in SARS-CoV-2 arose by recombination among bat viruses in nature. SARS-Cov-2 has a furin sequence P-R-R-A. The RmYN02 sequence has NO BASIC AMINO ACIDS!!! ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/be203c0696231cadcb1cb9020e35910c?s=50&d=mm&r=g) Vincent, I am curious about the question from “Michael” on 23 May 2020 at 11:06 am: “Can the furin cleavage insertion been accomplished using Crispr/Cas?” Please see the following links? Perhaps they may help to shed light on an answer? <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molcel.2019.09.013> <https://zlab.bio/cas13> Pingback: [The Evolution of Pandemics | FlybyNews](https://flybynews.wordpress.com/2020/03/20/the-evolution-of-pandemics/) ![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==) ![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/d9ba7572a7f5b0fa12c7f810bf82ac53?s=50&d=mm&r=g) RmYN02 has not insertions at the S1/S2 junction. The alignment in the paper is wrong. Comments are closed. sources.text: # [Press Releases](/press-releases) ## [Rep. Barr: COVID-19 Likely Leaked from Wuhan Virology Lab, Time to Ban Gain-of-Function Research](/press-releases?ID=BF264E31-F3E1-49F4-8056-F8C7AB3D3CB4) ### August 2, 2021 *![https://barr.house.gov/index.cfm?a=Files.Serve&File_id=1E6A0051-67AF-431C-9A9C-FE4A6A3F1D1D](https://barr.house.gov/index.cfm?a=Files.Serve&File_id=1E6A0051-67AF-431C-9A9C-FE4A6A3F1D1D)* ![https://barr.house.gov/index.cfm?a=Files.Serve&File_id=1E6A0051-67AF-431C-9A9C-FE4A6A3F1D1D](https://barr.house.gov/index.cfm?a=Files.Serve&File_id=1E6A0051-67AF-431C-9A9C-FE4A6A3F1D1D) *U.S. Representative Andy Barr (KY-06) pictured above during the House of Representatives China Task Force final report press conference in September of 2020.* **Washington, D.C.**— Today, House Foreign Affairs Committee (HFAC) Republicans released an [addendum](https://urldefense.com/v3/__https:/house.us12.list-manage.com/track/click?u=e585eec970e20145fb02e3825&id=6a8cd4e984&e=85f3fdb8de__;!!Bg5easoyC-OII2vlEqY8mTBrtW-N4OJKAQ!fgIkmqnvH5-MJ7XmZqOIiXENr90msF2PkAw8sMWmlmFHdqFRkILXvKYTpUScey1K2KBdyNNs$) to their [September 2020 report](https://urldefense.com/v3/__https:/house.us12.list-manage.com/track/click?u=e585eec970e20145fb02e3825&id=81bfbac46e&e=85f3fdb8de__;!!Bg5easoyC-OII2vlEqY8mTBrtW-N4OJKAQ!fgIkmqnvH5-MJ7XmZqOIiXENr90msF2PkAw8sMWmlmFHdqFRkILXvKYTpUScey1K2PcBA1K3$) on the origins of COVID-19. The addendum identified further evidence to indicate that the COVID-19 virus originated from the Wuhan Institute of Virology (WIV) Lab and the pandemic was likely started by an accidental lab leak. HFAC Republicans recommend a ban on federal funding for the genetic enhancement of viruses to make them more potent at least until an international standard for this research is set. This type of research is also known as “gain-of-function” research. “*For a year and a half, the Chinese Communist Party has manipulated, mislead, and deceived the world about the true origins of the COVID-19 virus,” said Representative Barr. “Today’s report disproves the Wuhan wet market story and lays out extensive evidence in support of the lab leak origins theory.* *“Over 600,000 Americans died from COVID-19. We will continue to work and deliver the truth to the American people about COVID-19,” Barr continued. “In the meantime, I join my fellow Republicans in calling for a ban on taxpayer funding of highly dangerous scientific research at least until international standards are set that prevent future outbreaks from occurring.”* **Highlights from The Origins of COVID-19 Report:** **Highlights from The Origins of COVID-19 Report Addendum:** There were significant concerns about the lax safety protocols in PRC labs, including from the head of the Chinese Center for Disease Control and Prevention and the head of the WIV’s BSL-4 lab. There were multiple requests for maintenance at the WIV, including in their new facility that had been operational for less than two years. Some of those requests included maintenance on “environmental air disinfection system” and “hazardous waste treatment system” – which would indicate concerns about how these systems meant to prevent lab leaks were functioning. Scientists at the WIV were conducting gain-of-function research at BSL-2 and BSL-3 safety levels. A BSL-2 lab has the same safety protocols as a dentist’s office. Scientists have been able to genetically modify coronaviruses without leaving a trace of that modification since 2005. Scientists at the WIV successfully modified coronaviruses without leaving a trace as early as 2016 – three years prior to the outbreak. Shi Zhengli, one of the lead virologists at the WIV known as the “batwoman” for her work with coronaviruses, has repeatedly lied about: Permalink: <https://barr.house.gov/2021/8/rep-barr-covid-19-likely-leaked-from-wuhan-virology-lab-time-to-ban-gain-of-function-research>

Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. ##### **2017-2021 ARCHIVED CONTENT** **You are viewing ARCHIVED CONTENT released online from January 20, 2017 to January 20, 2021.** **Content in this archive site is NOT UPDATED, and links may not function.** **For current information, go to [www.state.gov](https://www.state.gov/).** [Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/statedept) [Twitter](https://twitter.com/StateDept) [Instagram](https://www.instagram.com/statedept/) [YouTube](https://www.youtube.com/user/statevideo) [Flickr](https://www.flickr.com/photos/statephotos/) * [Menu](#nav__primary-nav) * [State Department Home](https://2017-2021.state.gov) # Fact Sheet: Activity at the Wuhan Institute of Virology Fact Sheet [Office of the Spokesperson](https://2017-2021.state.gov/bureaus-offices/under-secretary-for-public-diplomacy-and-public-affairs/bureau-of-global-public-affairs/office-of-the-spokesperson/) January 15, 2021 Share this page on: For more than a year, the Chinese Communist Party (CCP) has systematically prevented a transparent and thorough investigation of the COVID-19 pandemic’s origin, choosing instead to devote enormous resources to deceit and disinformation. Nearly two million people have died. Their families deserve to know the truth. Only through transparency can we learn what caused this pandemic and how to prevent the next one. The U.S. government does not know exactly where, when, or how the COVID-19 virus—known as SARS-CoV-2—was transmitted initially to humans. We have not determined whether the outbreak began through contact with infected animals or was the result of an accident at a laboratory in Wuhan, China. The virus could have emerged naturally from human contact with infected animals, spreading in a pattern consistent with a natural epidemic. Alternatively, a laboratory accident could resemble a natural outbreak if the initial exposure included only a few individuals and was compounded by asymptomatic infection. Scientists in China have researched animal-derived coronaviruses under conditions that increased the risk for accidental and potentially unwitting exposure. The CCP’s deadly obsession with secrecy and control comes at the expense of public health in China and around the world. The previously undisclosed information in this fact sheet, combined with open-source reporting, highlights three elements about COVID-19’s origin that deserve greater scrutiny: 1. Illnesses inside the Wuhan Institute of Virology (WIV): * The U.S. government has reason to believe that several researchers inside the WIV became sick in autumn 2019, before the first identified case of the outbreak, with symptoms consistent with both COVID-19 and common seasonal illnesses. This raises questions about the credibility of WIV senior researcher Shi Zhengli’s public claim that there was “zero infection” among the WIV’s staff and students of SARS-CoV-2 or SARS-related viruses. * Accidental infections in labs have caused several previous virus outbreaks in China and elsewhere, including a 2004 SARS outbreak in Beijing that infected nine people, killing one. * The CCP has prevented independent journalists, investigators, and global health authorities from interviewing researchers at the WIV, including those who were ill in the fall of 2019. Any credible inquiry into the origin of the virus must include interviews with these researchers and a full accounting of their previously unreported illness. 2. Research at the WIV: * Starting in at least 2016 – and with no indication of a stop prior to the COVID-19 outbreak – WIV researchers conducted experiments involving RaTG13, the bat coronavirus identified by the WIV in January 2020 as its closest sample to SARS-CoV-2 (96.2% similar). The WIV became a focal point for international coronavirus research after the 2003 SARS outbreak and has since studied animals including mice, bats, and pangolins. * The WIV has a published record of conducting “gain-of-function” research to engineer chimeric viruses. But the WIV has not been transparent or consistent about its record of studying viruses most similar to the COVID-19 virus, including “RaTG13,” which it sampled from a cave in Yunnan Province in 2013 after several miners died of SARS-like illness. * WHO investigators must have access to the records of the WIV’s work on bat and other coronaviruses before the COVID-19 outbreak. As part of a thorough inquiry, they must have a full accounting of why the WIV altered and then removed online records of its work with RaTG13 and other viruses. 3. Secret military activity at the WIV: * Secrecy and non-disclosure are standard practice for Beijing. For many years the United States has publicly raised concerns about China’s past biological weapons work, which Beijing has neither documented nor demonstrably eliminated, despite its clear obligations under the Biological Weapons Convention. * Despite the WIV presenting itself as a civilian institution, the United States has determined that the WIV has collaborated on publications and secret projects with China’s military. The WIV has engaged in classified research, including laboratory animal experiments, on behalf of the Chinese military since at least 2017. * The United States and other donors who funded or collaborated on civilian research at the WIV have a right and obligation to determine whether any of our research funding was diverted to secret Chinese military projects at the WIV. Today’s revelations just scratch the surface of what is still hidden about COVID-19’s origin in China. Any credible investigation into the origin of COVID-19 demands complete, transparent access to the research labs in Wuhan, including their facilities, samples, personnel, and records. As the world continues to battle this pandemic – and as WHO investigators begin their work, after more than a year of delays – the virus’s origin remains uncertain. The United States will continue to do everything it can to support a credible and thorough investigation, including by continuing to demand transparency on the part of Chinese authorities. [Bureau of East Asian and Pacific Affairs](https://2017-2021.state.gov/bureaus-offices/under-secretary-for-political-affairs/bureau-of-east-asian-and-pacific-affairs/) [China](https://2017-2021.state.gov/countries-areas/china/) [Coronavirus](https://2017-2021.state.gov/subjects/coronavirus/) [COVID-19](https://2017-2021.state.gov/subjects/covid-19/) [Global Health](https://2017-2021.state.gov/subjects/global-health/) [Office of the Spokesperson](https://2017-2021.state.gov/bureaus-offices/under-secretary-for-public-diplomacy-and-public-affairs/bureau-of-global-public-affairs/office-of-the-spokesperson/) ## U.S. Department *of* State #### The Lessons of 1989: Freedom and Our Future

✉ ∗Corresponding authors. yangjing@icdc.cn lushan@icdc.cn Received 2023 Jan 7; Accepted 2023 Apr 24; Collection date 2023. © 2023 The Authors This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/). [PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/) PMCID: PMC10151251 PMID: [37141989](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37141989/) ## [](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251)Abstract The current pandemic of COVID-19 caused by a novel coronavirus, severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus-2 (SARS-CoV-2), threatens human health around the world. Of particular concern is that bats are recognized as one of the most potential natural hosts of SARS-CoV-2; however, coronavirus ecology in bats is still nascent. Here, we performed a degenerate primer screening and next-generation sequencing analysis of 112 bats, collected from Hainan Province, China. Three coronaviruses, namely bat betacoronavirus (Bat CoV) CD35, Bat CoV CD36 and bat alphacoronavirus CD30 were identified. Bat CoV CD35 genome had 99.5% identity with Bat CoV CD36, both sharing the highest nucleotide identity with Bat Hp-betacoronavirus Zhejiang2013 (71.4%), followed by SARS-CoV-2 (54.0%). Phylogenetic analysis indicated that Bat CoV CD35 formed a distinct clade, and together with Bat Hp-betacoronavirus Zhejiang2013, was basal to the lineage of SARS-CoV-1 and SARS-CoV-2. Notably, Bat CoV CD35 harbored a canonical furin-like S1/S2 cleavage site that resembles the corresponding sites of SARS-CoV-2. The furin cleavage sites between CD35 and CD36 are identical. In addition, the receptor-binding domain of Bat CoV CD35 showed a highly similar structure to that of SARS-CoV-1 and SARS-CoV-2, especially in one binding loop. In conclusion, this study deepens our understanding of the diversity of coronaviruses and provides clues about the natural origin of the furin cleavage site of SARS-CoV-2. ## [](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251)Abstract •Full-length genome sequences of alphacoronavirus and betacoronavirus were identified from bats in Hainan Province, China.•The betacoronavirus is a distant relative of SARS-CoV-2, but contains a polybasic furin-like cleavage site.•The receptor-binding domain of the betacoronavirus (Bat CD35) was similar to that of SARS-CoV-1 and SARS-CoV-2. **Keywords:**Bat, Coronavirus, SARS-CoV-2, Betacoronavirus, Furin, Cleavage site ## [](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251)Introduction 1 The ongoing pandemic of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) underscores that some coronaviruses (CoVs) are highly transmissible, which remains a major threat to human health and public safety ([Jiang and Shi, 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib13); [Singh et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib27); [Hu et al., 2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib11)). CoVs belong to the family _Coronaviridae_, which is divided into four genera _Alphacoronavirus_, _Betacoronavirus_, _Gammacoronavirus_, and _Deltacoronavirus_ ([Hu et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib12), [2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib11); [Zhu et al., 2021a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib36)). CoVs are a group of single-stranded positive-sense RNA viruses that can infect a wide range of animals including humans, causing mild to lethal diseases ([Frutos et al., 2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib7)). Over the past 20 years, seven CoVs, which can cause human diseases, have been identified, including human coronavirus (HCoV)-NL63 belonging to genus _Alphacoronavirus_, severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus (SARS-CoV-1), HCoV-HKU1, Middle East respiratory syndrome coronavirus (MERS-CoV), and SARS-CoV-2 belonging to the genus _Betacoronavirus_ ([Frutos et al., 2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib7)). The structure of SARS-CoV-2 spike (S) protein, which determines the binding affinity to the host-cell receptor, plays an important role in viral infections and also determines the host range of the virus ([Letko et al., 2020a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib19); [Li MY et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib24)). After binding to the host-cell receptor, the S protein is cleaved by a variety of cellular proteases, including furin, at an S1/S2 cleavage site [motif (**R**/**K**)-(2X)n-(**R**/**K**)↓, where/= or, X​=​any amino acid (aa), n​=​0, 1, 2, or 3 spacer aa, ↓​=​cleavage site, bold font​=​key aa], producing the S1 and S2 subunits, which determine the cellular tropism and pathogenesis of the virus ([Duckert et al., 2004](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib6); [Coutard et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib5); [Letko et al., 2020a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib19)). The receptor-binding domain (RBD) within the S1 subunit can fold independently of the rest of the S protein and is the determining component for the virus to entry host cells ([Letko et al., 2020a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib19)). The S2 protein has another proteolytic site (S2') downstream of S1/S2 cleavage site, which is also important for virus entry process ([Coutard et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib5)). Although the origin of SARS-CoV-2 remains unknown, accumulating evidence indicates that SARS-CoV-2 originates from bats naturally, including the fact that the closest relatives of SARS-CoV-2 are bat coronaviruses from Yunnan Province of China and Lao PDR ([Letko et al., 2020b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib20); [Zhou P et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib34); [Li et al., 2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib23)). To investigate the origin, it is essential to track the diversity of animal coronaviruses, particularly the bat coronaviruses, and identify the potential sequences contributing to SARS-CoV-2 mosaicism ([Temmam et al., 2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib29)). Bats exhibit enormous diversity, with more than 1400 species inhabiting different environments ([Burgin et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib3)). During gaining their unique traits (the ability to fly) among mammals, bats seem to have acquired a variety of antiviral immune response genes that limit self-damaging inflammation ([Frutos et al., 2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib7)). Bats are important reservoir hosts for a variety of zoonotic viruses ([Chen et al., 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib4)), and have acquired increasing attention after being implicated in SARS-CoV-2 related viruses (SARSr-CoV-2), including RaTG13, RmYN02, ZC45, PrC31, BANAL-20-52, BANAL-20-103, and BANAL-20-236 ([Hu et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib12); [Zhou P et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib34); [Li et al., 2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib23); [Temmam et al., 2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib29)). However, none of the SARSr-CoV-2 harbors the furin-like cleavage site within the S protein, which is associated with enhanced pathogenicity in humans and present in all the sequenced SARS-CoV-2. The investigation of coronaviruses circulating in bats may provide vital clues to trace the origin of SARS-CoV-2 and to identify the unknown pathogens. In this study, we present the genetic characterizations of a novel bat betacoronavirus with a furin-like cleavage site from Baoting County, Hainan Province, China. ## [](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251)Materials and methods 2 ### Sample collection 2.1 Between March 30 and April 1, 2021, 112 bats were captured using nylon netting from two abandoned caves (18°24′N, 109°36′E) in Baoting County, Hainan Province, China ([Zhu et al., 2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib35)). The captured bats were put into airtight bags and anesthetized with ether. The bats were dissected, and their intestinal tissue specimens were collected. Due to biosafety, to prevent contamination, all dissecting experiments were conducted in a biosafety cabinet of Baoting Center for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). Then, the samples were transported to the National Institute for Communicable Disease Control and Prevention of China CDC and stored at −80​°C until further use. The bat species were confirmed by morphology and PCR amplification targeting NADH dehydrogenase subunit 1 ([Ge et al., 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib8)). ### Screening of coronaviruses 2.2 The total RNA of each sample was extracted using the RNeasy Plus Universal Mini Kit (QIAGEN, Düsseldorf, Germany) according to the manufacturer's instruction. The pan-coronavirus reverse transcription (RT)-PCR targeting a 440-bp fragment of the RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (_RdRp_) gene of CoVs was used for CoV screening ([Lau et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib18); [Zhu et al., 2021b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib37)). RT-PCR was performed as previously described ([Zhu et al., 2021c](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib38)), and PCR products were gel purified and subjected to Sanger sequencing. The obtained sequences were blasted against the NCBI non-redundant protein database. ### Next-generation sequencing and whole genome confirmation 2.3 The total RNA of the positive sample was selected for next-generation sequencing (NGS) on the Illumina HiSeq2000 platform. The nucleic acids were digested using DNase I, and rRNA was removed using the Ribo-Zero Gold rRNA Removal (Epidemiology) Kit. Libraries were then constructed with Truseq Stranded Total RNA Library Prep Gold Kit (Illumina, San Diego, USA) following the manufacturer's instructions. The raw data was subjected to quality control to remove adapters, low-quality reads, and rRNA sequences. The clean data were used for _de novo_ assembly using Trinity ([Grabherr et al., 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib9)), and the obtained contigs were annotated using Diamond in non-redundant protein database with an E-value of 1​×​10−5 ([Buchfink et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib2)). Based on the NGS genome, paired primers ([Supplementary Table S1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#appsec1)) were designed to amplify the different fragments of the genome in all positive samples. The PCR products were sequenced and aligned to confirm the genomic sequence. ### Phylogenetic analysis 2.4 The open reading frames (ORFs) were searched by comparing the sequences with those of other coronaviruses and using ORFfinder with default parameters ([https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/orffinder/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/orffinder/)). Signal peptides and the protein family were predicted using SignalP and InterProScan ([Quevillon et al., 2005](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib25); [Almagro Armenteros et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib1)), respectively. The nucleotide and aa sequences were aligned using MAFFT ([Katoh and Standley, 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib15)). The most appropriate substitution models were confirmed by ModelFinder ([Kalyaanamoorthy et al., 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib14)). Phylogenetic trees were built using PhyML 3.0 with 1000 bootstrap replicates, with infectious bronchitis virus (IBV) used as the outgroup, and visualized in iTOL ([Guindon et al., 2010](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib10); [Letunic and Bork, 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib21)). The accession numbers used in this study were labeled in the bracket after the corresponding taxa in [Fig.1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#fig1).Fig.1Genomic characteristics and identity. **A** Schematic diagram of the genomes of Bat CoV CD35 and related CoVs. Step, 150; Window, 1500. **B** Nucleotide sequence identities between Bat CoV CD35 with representative CoVs. The similarity was calculated using T-RECs software. Bat Hp-betaCoV Zhejiang2013, Bat Hp-betacoronavirus Zhejiang2013; SARS-CoV-2 WIV02, Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 WIV02; SARS-CoV-1 BJ01, Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 1 BJ01; Pangolin CoV GX-P5L, pangolin/GX/P5L/2017; Bat CoV RaTG13, Bat Coronavirus RaTG13; Bat CoV RmYN02, BetaCoV/Rm/Yunnan/YN02/2019; Bat SARS CoV HKU3, Bat SARS coronavirus HKU3.Fig.1 ### Structural analysis 2.5 The furin cleavage sites and scores were estimated by the ProP server (v1.0) with default parameters ([Duckert et al., 2004](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib6)). The RBD three-dimensional structures were modeled by SWISS-MODEL with the RBD structure of SARS-CoV-2 (PDB: 7a91.1) as the template ([Waterhouse et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib31); [Yang et al., 2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib32)). The model results were visualized using PyMOL 2.4 program ([https://pymol.org/2/#opensource](https://pymol.org/2/#opensource)). ## [](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251)Results 3 ### Identification of CoVs in bats 3.1 A total of 112 intestinal tissues of bats were collected and used to screen CoVs with a pan-coronavirus RT-PCR approach ([Lau et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib18)). Results showed that seven (7/112, 6.3%) intestinal samples were positive for coronaviruses. Sequencing of the amplification products (a 440-bp fragment in the _RdRp_ genes) revealed that these seven sequences belonged to two lineages. Among them, six (6/112, 5.4%) _RdRp_ partial sequences shared homology with betacoronaviruses, with a 100% nucleotide identity to each other, which were sampled from _Hipposideros armiger_ (_H.armiger_). The remaining sequence was from _H.pomona_ (sample ID: CD30), showing highly homology to an alphacoronavirus (_Bat coronavirus HKU10_). ### Genomic characteristics 3.2 To better understand the genomic features, almost full-length genome sequences were obtained using NGS. The strain from _H.pomona_ (sample ID: CD30) was named as bat alphacoronavirus CD30 (accession number [OK017907](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/OK017907)), sharing 83.0%–86.0% genome identity and 92.8%–93.1% aa identity in ORF1ab with the corresponding sequences of _Bat coronavirus_ HKU10 species ([Lau et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib18)). Another two viruses identified from two of those six positive samples of _H.armiger_ were tentatively named as bat betacoronavirus CD35 (Bat CoV CD35, accession number [OK017908](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/OK017908)) and Bat CoV CD36 (accession number [OQ469322](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/OQ469322)), respectively. Based on the genome from NGS, all six positive samples were further verified by RT-PCR using paired primers ([Supplementary Table S1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#appsec1)) targeting multiple genomic sites. The results suggested that CoVs from the six positive samples belonged to the same betacoronavirus, indicative of the most dominant CoV circulating in the two bat colonies. Bat CoV CD35 was selected for following analyses, which shared 99.5% nucleotide identity with Bat CoV CD36 based on almost full-length genome sequence. The almost full-length genome of Bat CoV CD35 contained 31,252 nucleotides, with a G​+​C content of 41% ([Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#tbl1)). The genomic organization of Bat CoV CD35 was identical to that of Bat Hp-betacoronavirus (Bat Hp-betaCoV) Zhejiang2013, and was similar to those of SARS-CoV-2 WIV02 and SARS-CoV-1 BJ01, except that the ORF2 protein was absent in SARS-CoV-2 WIV02 and SARS-CoV-1 BJ01 ([Supplementary Table S2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#appsec1) and [Fig.1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#fig1)A). The full genome of Bat CoV CD35 shared the highest nucleotide identity with Bat Hp-betaCoV Zhejiang2013 (71.4%), followed by SARS-CoV-2 WIV02 (54.0%) ([Fig.1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#fig1)B and [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#tbl1)). In the conserved replicase domains, Bat CoV CD35 shared 68.3%–91.5% aa identity in the 3C-like protease (3CLpro) region, 77.0%–93.1% in the RdRp region, and 81.0%–89.2% in the helicase (Hel) region with the corresponding sequences of Bat Hp-betaCoV Zhejiang2013, SARS-CoV-2, SARS-CoV-1, SARSr-CoV-1 (HKU3), and SARSr-CoV-2 (GX-P5L, RaTG13, and RmYN02) ([Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#tbl1)).Table 1The genomic information and sequence identity of Bat CoV CD35 with representative CoVs.Table 1CoronavirusesGenome size (nt)G​+​C content (%)Pairwise identity (%)Genome (nt)3CLpro (aa)RdRp (aa)Hel (aa)S (aa)E (aa)M (aa)N (aa)Bat Hp-betaCoV Zhejiang201331,49141.371.491.593.189.270.076.079.476.1SARS-CoV-2 WIV0229,82538.054.069.077.281.536.859.552.750.4Pangolin CoV GX-P5L29,80638.553.468.378.081.036.259.552.749.9Bat CoV RaTG1329,85538.053.968.677.081.736.759.553.450.4Bat CoV RmYN0229,67138.253.568.377.081.536.459.553.450.4SARS-CoV-1 BJ0129,72540.852.869.077.681.536.460.853.949.3Bat SARS CoV HKU329,72841.152.868.677.681.736.660.854.349.0SARSr-CoV-1: HKU3. SARSr-CoV-2: GX-P5L, RaTG13, and RmYN02. Bat Hp-betaCoV, Bat Hp-betacoronavirus Zhejiang2013; SARS-CoV, Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus. Pangolin CoV GX-P5L, pangolin/GX/P5L/2017; Bat CoV RaTG13, Bat Coronavirus RaTG13; Bat CoV RmYN02, BetaCoV/Rm/Yunnan/YN02/2019; Bat SARS CoV HKU3, Bat SARS coronavirus HKU3. Unexpectedly, the S protein of Bat CoV CD35 was significantly distinct from its related CoVs ([Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#tbl1)), showing only 70% aa identity to Bat Hp-betaCoV Zhejiang2013, and far lower aa identity (<40%) to SARSr-CoV-2 and SARSr-CoV-1. For envelope (E), membrane (M), and nucleocapsid (N) proteins, Bat CoV CD35 showed relatively higher aa identity with its relatives, while sharing the highest similarity to the corresponding protein of Bat Hp-betaCoV Zhejiang2013 ([Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#tbl1)). ### Phylogenetic analyses 3.3 Phylogenetic analysis based on the complete genome revealed that bat alphacoronavirus CD30 fall within genus _Alphacoronavirus_ and was grouped with the members within _Bat coronavirus_ HKU10 species ([Fig.2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#fig2) and [Supplementary Fig.S1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#appsec1)). Meanwhile, Bat CoV CD35 belonged to the genus _Betacoronavirus_ and formed a separate clade grouping together with Bat Hp-betaCoV Zhejiang2013, which was basal to the cluster of SARSr-CoV-1 (HKU3) and SARSr-CoV-2 (GX-P5L, RaTG13, and RmYN02) ([Fig.2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#fig2)). In addition, phylogenetic trees based on the aa sequences of ORF1ab, S, M, and N proteins ([Fig.3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#fig3)) showed similar topological structures. The results also revealed that the closest relative of Bat CoV CD35 was Bat Hp-betaCoV Zhejiang2013, both sharing ancestors with SARSr-CoV-1 (HKU3) and SARSr-CoV-2 (GX-P5L, RaTG13, and RmYN02). The results of the evolutionary analyses were identical to that of the similarity plot, suggesting that Bat CoV CD35 was most closely related to Bat Hp-BetaCoV Zhejiang2013, followed by SARSr-CoV-2 and SARSr-CoV-1. The absence of discrepancies in clades among the phylogenetic trees and the values in [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#tbl1) indicate that there may be no recombination events for Bat CoV CD35. Indeed, the recombination analysis based on the genomes of related CoVs did not detect recombination events for Bat CoV CD35.Fig.2Phylogenetic analysis based on genome sequences. IBV, infectious bronchitis virus; SARS-CoV, SARS coronavirus; MERS-CoV, MERS coronavirus; MHV, mouse hepatitis virus.Fig.3 ### Furin-like cleavage site 3.4 During infection, the S protein of SARS-CoV-2 must be cleaved by host furin-like proteases at both the S1/S2 and S2′ cleavage sites, which play a significant role in viral entry into cells ([Coutard et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib5)). The furin-like S1/S2 cleavage site in Bat CoV CD35 had the **R**XX**R**↓ motif with position (P)1 and P4 as basic residues, which were identical to those of SARS-CoV-2, MERS-CoV, HCoV-HKU1, and HCoV-OC43 ([Table 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#tbl2) and [Supplementary Fig.S2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#appsec1)). Based on experimental results derived from the literatures, the scores of cleavage sites could be estimated using artificial neural networks that were calculated by 4-fold cross-validation. The higher the score (between zero and one), the more confident the prediction is ([Duckert et al., 2004](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib6)). The score of the S1/S2 cleavage site for Bat CoV CD35 was estimated at 0.646 ([Table 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#tbl2)), which was higher than those of SARS-CoV-2 (0.620), MERS-CoV (0.563), HCoV-OC43 (0.551), and Bat Hp-betaCoV Zhejiang2013 (0.608) but lower than that of HCoV-HKU1 (0.882). However, the motif of the S2′ cleavage site in Bat CoV CD35 was replaced with a monobasic motif (**R**↓S), which was slightly different from those of SARS-CoV-1 and SARS-CoV-2 but was identical to other human CoVs, including MERS-CoV, HCoV-OC43, HCoV-NL63, and HCoV-229E ([Table 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#tbl2)), suggesting a higher restriction or a less efficient cleavage by furin proteases ([Coutard et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib5)). S2' cleavage site scores of Bat CoV CD35 and SARS-CoV-1 were almost the same, which were lower than that of SARS-CoV-2 ([Table 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#tbl2)). In addition, according to the (**R**/**K**)-(2X)n-(**R**/**K**)↓ motif (when n​=​0 and 1) and monobasic motif, the polybasic (furin-like) S1/S2 cleavage site in SARS-CoV-2 (**R**↓**R**↓A**R**↓) was also present in Bat CoV CD35 (**R**↓A**K**↓**R**↓), which was absent in all relatives of SARS-CoV-2, including pangolin coronaviruses (GX/P5E and GX/P5L) and bat-origin coronaviruses (BANAL-20-52, BANAL-20-103, and BANAL-20-236 from Laos, RaTG13 and PrC31 from China) ([Coutard et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib5); [Lam et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib17); [Zhou P et al., 2020](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib34); [Li et al., 2021](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib23); [Temmam et al., 2022](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10151251#bib29)).
